title: Sundew and the Ghosts
summary: In the village of Skruväng, deep in the forests of Småland, there lived a girl named Sundew—called sårfot in the old tongue because her grandmother said she was as delicate and as keen as the tiny carnivorous plant that grew in the boggy meadows.
full_text:
In the village of Skruväng, deep in the forests of Småland, there lived a girl named Sundew—called sårfot in the old tongue because her grandmother said she was as delicate and as keen as the tiny carnivorous plant that grew in the boggy meadows. What made Sundew truly different was not her name or her gentle nature, but her sight. From the time she was a small child, she could see what others could not—the spirits of the dead who walked the mists of twilight.

The village folk were uneasy around her. They whispered that she was cursed or blessed, depending on who spoke. But Sundew was not afraid of the ghosts. To her, they were simply people—people who had unfinished business, people who were lost, people who needed help.

One autumn evening, Sundew was walking home through the forest when she saw a woman standing by an ancient oak tree. The woman's form flickered like candle flame in wind, and her eyes were filled with desperate sorrow.

Have you seen my son?' the ghost whispered. He was taken from me before I could name him. I have searched for so long.'

Sundew, though only twelve winters old, did not run. Instead, she sat with the ghost and listened. The woman told her of a child left in the woods many years ago, before the village had a proper churchyard. Sundew promised to help.

It took her many years—years of asking old women in the village, of digging through church records, of walking the forest paths at dusk. Finally, she found the unmarked grave of a baby near a standing stone. She told the priest, and the child was given a proper burial in hallowed ground.

The next time Sundew walked in the forest at twilight, the woman was there—but her face was peaceful now. Thank you,' she said, and faded into golden light.

Another winter, a man appeared to Sundew. He was ragged, with earth still clinging to his clothes—buried without funeral rites, she knew. He could not rest because his family did not know where he lay. Sundew found his grave in an unmarked plot and told his descendants. They held a blessing, and the man smiled his gratitude before departing.

This is why we remember: ghosts need help, too. And why we leave offerings at crossroads—to help the lost find their way. Sundew grew old in that village, never afraid, always helping. When she died, they say she did not become a ghost at all—her kindness had earned her a different path. But on quiet evenings in Småland, people still leave small offerings at the edges of forests, just in case a wandering soul needs help.
moral: Even ghosts need help.
source: Swedish Folklore

title: Trolls in Church
summary: In the mountains of Norway, where the trolls have their halls in the great stone halls, there live creatures who see humans as pests—annoying, but sometimes useful.
full_text:
In the mountains of Norway, where the trolls have their halls in the great stone halls, there live creatures who see humans as pests—annoying, but sometimes useful. What the trolls cannot understand is why humans need churches, those small stone buildings where they gather to sing and speak of gods. Some trolls are curious; some are envious. And some, the tales say, wanted what the humans had.

It was well-known in the valley villages that trolls could not enter a church. The holy sign above the door, the consecrated ground within—these were barriers that no troll could cross. But curiosity drives even the mountain folk, and one midwinter's eve, a troll-woman decided to see for herself.

She wrapped herself in a great white sheet—thinking to look like a human woman come to worship. The sexton, an old man named Lars, saw her come in. Even in the candlelight, he could see she was huge—her shadow filled the whole doorway. Her face, though passing for human, had something wrong about it.

She gave him a bundle—a child, she said, to be christened. But Lars saw, in the flickering light, that the child was not right. It was heavy, and its skin was cold. He baptised it anyway, using Gods name. The child screamed—a terrible sound, more animal than human—then went still. It was a changeling, a troll child left to be raised as human. But the baptism had killed it. The troll-woman fled into the night, and Lars was never troubled again.

Another tale tells of trolls who grew bold enough to attack. They came down from the mountains one Sunday morning, their stone feet shaking the earth, determined to tear down the little church that dared to stand on their mountain. But the priest was quick. He rang the great iron bell—the sanctus bell, rung during mass. The sound, blessed and holy, struck the trolls like a physical blow. Where they stood, they became stone. To this day, there are strange stone formations on the mountain slopes that the folk call the Trolls' Church.

This is why churches are safe against trolls—the divine protects its own. And why bells matter: ringing them drives back the darkness. The trolls of Norway, for all their strength, cannot stand against the power of holiness.
moral: The divine protects.
source: Norwegian Folklore

title: When Mother Troll Took in the King's Washing
summary: Troll Mother and her son Drulle live in a forest cottage where she does laundry for travelers. When a king builds a nearby castle for his queen and newborn princess, the lady-in-waiting hires Troll Mother to wash the royal clothes cheaply so she can pocket the difference. Troll Mother becomes enchanted by the beautiful baby clothes and begins stealing them, using a troll rhyme her son recites during counting to conceal the theft. An orphan girl named Inga, who mends clothes at the castle, is blamed for the missing garments and sent away. She wanders to the trolls' cottage and is taken in, where Drulle becomes infatuated with her. Inga meets the queen's young page in the forest, who believes in her innocence and asks for her hand, but she refuses out of shame. When Troll Mother reveals the stolen baby clothes and her tail becomes loose, Inga realizes she is living with trolls and flees. She marries the page, while Drulle's misery causes him to forget the counting rhyme, leading to exposure of the theft. The trolls abandon their cottage, and years later return the remaining baby clothes. Inga and the page live happily, becoming the royal forester and his wife.
moral: Honesty and kindness prevail over greed and deception.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, translated by Holger Lundbergh, Delacorte Press/S. Lawrence, 1973. Originally published in Swedish as 'När trollmor skötte kungens storbyk' (1914) by Elsa Beskow.

title: The Magician's Cape
summary: An evil magician lives in a mountain palace filled with frightened young girls he has stolen from their families. He forces them to dance and sing for him, and when one displeases him, he casts her into the forest to be devoured by bears and wolves. The magician possesses a black flying cape: when a maiden steps onto it, the cape turns into wings and he whisks her away. One day he discovers Alvida, a smith's beautiful daughter, combing her golden hair at a window. He tries to ensnare her with his cape, but she is clever and cautious. When a ram (summoned by the magician's whistle) charges at her, Alvida stumbles and tears the cape with the ram's horns. Feeling remorseful, she mends the tear using two thorns as a needle and a strand of her golden hair as thread. The magician then tricks her into stepping onto the cape, and she is carried away. As she cries for help, her golden hair catches on a tree branch and refuses to break. The magician is forced to release her. Enraged, he returns to his castle, but finds he cannot sleep—the golden thread sewn into his cape glows so brightly it fills every room with light. No matter how he tries to hide the cape, the radiance follows him. Desperate, he returns to Alvida's village and offers gifts, but she refuses to remove the thread, knowing trolls and magicians cannot enter Christian homes. Every good deed the magician performs dims the light slightly, and he is forced into a life of reluctant kindness. The golden thread represents the power of good deeds over evil ones.
moral: A good deed shines brighter than any magic, and even evil can be tempered by kindness.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, translated by Holger Lundbergh, 1973. Originally published as 'Trollkarlens kappa' (1912) by Anna Wahlenberg, in the annual 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Barrel Bung
summary: Famine has struck the parish of Bolinge. All the cows have gone dry except for those belonging to the rich, elderly Lady Skinflint and her ugly daughter, who somehow have hundreds of cheeses and tubs of butter from only four cows. When Jerker, the eldest son of the poor farmer Jonson, discovers that his family's cow Star has stopped giving milk, he decides to investigate. He stays awake and catches the family tomte (house spirit), who tells him the answer lies in Lady Skinflint's storeroom. Jerker trades his Sunday clothes for food and offers himself as a farmhand at the manor. He breaks the lock on the storeroom and hides to observe. That night, he watches Lady Skinflint take out a large barrel bung (stopper), wedge it into a hole in the wall, and milk the udders of the villagers' cows that magically appear through the bung. Enraged, Jerker recites a rhyme that causes a cow's leg to kick out from the bung instead, beating the old woman and her daughter until they beg for mercy. Jerker forces them to hand over all the gold they earned from stealing milk and takes the magic bung. He returns the gold to the villagers, who share it equally, rewarding Jerker with a portion that allows him to buy a farm and marry his sweetheart. He destroys the bung in a fire so it can never harm anyone again.
moral: Greed and theft will be punished, and cleverness and courage prevail over injustice.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Tunntappen' (1903) by Anna Wahlenberg, later in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Seven Wishes
summary: Olle Niklasson is a thirteen-year-old boy who is exceptionally ugly, lazy, and stupid. His hair resembles a dried juniper bush, his cheeks like swollen fly agaric mushrooms, and he is so lazy he barely moves when he falls. Even the magpies laugh at him. One day in the forest, Olle sees a viper staring down a frog that is slowly crawling toward the snake's open mouth. Too stupid and lazy to intervene with a stick, he nonetheless shouts at the frog for being foolish. This breaks the snake's spell, and the frog escapes. The frog is revealed to be a fairy queen who had been punished for mocking a frog in danger — she was transformed into a frog for twenty-four hours. Grateful for her rescue, she grants Olle seven wishes. Olle uses his wishes foolishly — his first wish makes his bundle of sticks fly wildly through the village, destroying crops and frightening animals. He wastes wish after wish on trivial, selfish things. But after using up six wishes with nothing to show for it, he finally wishes, almost accidentally, that he could learn to read. The fairy queen grants this wish, but tells him that true wisdom comes only through his own hard work. Olle discovers that reading opens his mind. He becomes diligent, studious, and kind, eventually growing into a good and handsome man whom everyone loves. The story teaches that laziness and ignorance are the true sources of ugliness, while knowledge and hard work bring beauty and happiness.
moral: True happiness comes not from magical wishes but from self-improvement through reading and hard work.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'De sju önskningarna' (1907) by Alfred Smedberg, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The King's Choice
summary: King Gustav must leave his country on a long journey and needs to choose one of his six councilors to govern in his absence — but each has a flaw: one is too bossy, another too young, one drinks too much, one is obsessed with money, another with his appearance, and the last is simply uninspiring. Riding through the forest, the king encounters a fairy who rises from the river mist. She transforms him into a woodcutter and places him in a cottage by the river. The fairy arranges for the six councilors' boat to be caught in a storm, forcing them to seek shelter at the woodcutter's cottage. Disguised as a poor woodcutter, the king invites them to dinner and observes their true natures when they believe no one of consequence is watching. Through this test, he discovers which councilor possesses genuine inner strength, humility, and wisdom. The story explores themes of leadership, the difference between appearance and reality, and the value of looking beneath the surface to find true merit.
moral: True leadership is revealed not by titles or appearances, but by character tested in humble circumstances.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Kungens val' by Anna Wahlenberg, included in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Four Big Trolls and Little Peter Pastureman
summary: When the old Troll King dies, four enormous trolls — each as large as a mountain — vie for the throne. Their vicious rivalry threatens all magical folk in the kingdom, as their power struggles cause chaos throughout the land. Little Peter, a brave and lighthearted shepherd boy (called Vill-Vallareman in Swedish) who works as a pastureman for the four trolls, finds himself caught in the middle of their conflict. Despite his small size, Peter possesses quick wits and a courageous heart. While the four giant trolls argue, scheme, and battle one another, Peter uses his intelligence to outmaneuver them. He blows on a birchbark horn, which plays a key role in the resolution of the trolls' dispute. Through his bravery and clever thinking, Peter prevents what would have been a tragic outcome for all the magical folk. The story, richly illustrated by John Bauer with iconic images of the trolls and the small shepherd boy, demonstrates that size and strength matter less than intelligence and courage. It is a charming blend of adventure and folklore that teaches the triumph of good over evil through wit rather than force.
moral: Intelligence and courage can overcome even the greatest physical strength.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Sagan om de fyra stortrollen och lille Vill-Vallareman' (1909) by Cyrus Granér, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Troll Ride
summary: Peder Lars, a farmer's son, is riding to town to buy a new jacket before courting the beautiful and wealthy Lisa that evening. He is confident she will accept his proposal. But on the way through the forest, he encounters a hideous troll-woman lying injured in a ditch. She begs him to collect resin from seven pine trees to heal her wounded leg, offering gold coins in return. Peder Lars is repulsed by her appearance and initially refuses, but the haunting cries of the troll-woman's mother calling for her drive him to collect the resin. He tosses it at her in irritation and rides on. The delay causes him to be late: while en route, his brother meets him with terrible news — the rich miller Jonas has also come to propose to Lisa. With only minutes to spare and miles to go, Peder Lars' horse suddenly gains supernatural speed. Unseen, the grateful troll-woman rides behind him, her magic urging the horse forward at breakneck speed through the dark forest. Though his jacket is torn and his hair is wild, Peder Lars arrives at Lisa's house at the very last moment. Lisa, seeing his determined and disheveled appearance, finds him handsome and rushes into his arms. Peder Lars wins her hand, and from that day forward, he is never late again — because the troll-woman's magic ensures he always arrives on time.
moral: Even a reluctant act of kindness can be rewarded in unexpected ways.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Trollritten' (1910) by Anna Wahlenberg, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Trolls and the Youngest Tomte
summary: In the world of Swedish tomtar (house gnomes), the youngest and most curious tomte listens with wonder as the older tomtar tell stories of their adventurous visits inside the trolls' mountain. The older tomtar warn of the dangers, but the youngest tomte's curiosity gets the better of him. He decides to venture into the trolls' mountain himself, despite the warnings. Inside the mountain, he discovers an open treasure chest guarded by two terrible trolls. The young tomte must use all his wits and courage to navigate the perilous troll realm and escape with his life. He narrowly avoids being captured and uses clever tricks to outsmart the trolls, who are large and powerful but not very intelligent. The story, beautifully illustrated by John Bauer (whose artwork for this tale is among his most celebrated), captures the tension between youthful curiosity and the wisdom of experience. The youngest tomte's adventure teaches respect for the dangers of the world while also celebrating the bravery required to explore the unknown. He eventually escapes with barely his life intact, learning a valuable lesson about the importance of heeding the warnings of elders.
moral: Curiosity can lead to dangerous adventures, and it is wise to listen to the counsel of those with more experience.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Trollen och tomtepojken' (1909) by Alfred Smedberg, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Ring
summary: A young prince rides out in the moonlight and finds a tiny ring by the shore — a delicate band with blue stones arranged like a forget-me-not flower. He picks it up and puts it in his pocket. The ring belongs to a captive princess who, as a child, was taken from her mountain castle when a foreign conqueror killed her father and imprisoned her. Now, the foreign prince wants to marry her, but he is a coarse, giant-like brute. Desperate to escape, the princess had thrown the ring into the river as a plea for help, following a spell her mother taught her. The ring leads the prince to her. Meanwhile, a mountain troll has also been pursuing the princess, intending to eat her (keeping her fattened up for years). When the prince discovers the princess imprisoned in the troll's mountain domain, he must face both the mountain troll and the foreign prince to free her. He and the princess escape on his horse, with the mountain troll bellowing after them like a pack of wolves. They ride through forests and across rivers until they reach the prince's castle. The story is notable for its romantic opening (described as having 'a perfect fairy tale first line') and its layered conflicts — the innocent ring, the captive princess, the hungry troll, and the prince's steadfast pursuit of love and justice. John Bauer's illustration of the knight riding with the princess is one of his most famous works.
moral: Love and courage can overcome even the most terrible obstacles.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Ringen' (1914) by Helena Nyblom, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll' (Vol. 8).

title: The Old Troll of Big Mountain
summary: A poor cottager and his wife own little more than a small cottage, two goats, and their five-year-old son Olle. While they work as day laborers, they lock Olle in the cottage with bread and milk, letting the goats graze in an enclosure. One evening, they return to find both goats gone — the evil old troll of Big Mountain (Storberget) has stolen them. The loss is devastating, and Olle now gets only water instead of goat milk. Worse, they fear the troll might return for Olle himself. Determined to recover what was taken, Olle sets out despite his young age. Armed with a board with a nail and a wooden sword, he ventures into the forest to confront the troll. The old troll of Big Mountain is a fearsome creature — described in Swedish folklore as a pipe-smoking, flute-playing giant who steals livestock and children. Olle's courage in facing this terrifying being demonstrates that even the smallest and weakest can stand against oppression. The story, originally published in the magazine Tummeliten in 1900 with illustrations by Jenny Nyström, presents a classic tale of a child's bravery against a supernatural adversary, with the troll representing the harsh and unpredictable dangers of the natural world that rural Swedish families had to contend with.
moral: Even the smallest and youngest can find the courage to face great dangers.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Trollgubben i Storberget' (1900) by Anna Wahlenberg, in 'Länge, länge, sedan... Sagor'.

title: Leap the Elk and Little Princess Cottongrass
summary: Princess Cottongrass (Tuvstarr) has lived her entire life in the Dream Castle, a sheltered world of safety and beauty. One day, a magnificent elk named Long-Leap (Skutt) appears. Enchanted by his stately bearing and crown-like antlers, she begs him to carry her out into the world. The elk warns her: 'The world is big and cold, little child, and you are so small. The world is full of evil and wickedness, and it will hurt you.' But she insists, and he relents. On their journey through the deep Swedish forest, the princess encounters a series of supernatural dangers. First, fairies lure her, and in her distraction, she loses her golden crown. Then the Lady of the Forest (Skogsrå) tricks her into letting go of the elk's antlers to show her dress — and snatches it away, leaving her naked. Each time, the elk warns her to hold tight to his antlers, but her innocent curiosity and trust lead her to lose more of herself. Finally, they reach a dark tarn deep in the forest. Mesmerized by the dark, still water, Princess Cottongrass leans forward to see her reflection, and the golden heart her mother gave her at birth slips from her neck and sinks into the depths. Despite the elk's desperate warnings, she cannot tear herself away from the water. The elk, heartbroken, leaves her there. The story ends with a poignant transformation: the princess becomes a small white cottongrass flower, forever leaning over the edge of the pool, gazing into the water for her lost heart. The elk returns now and then, the only one who remembers she was once a princess. John Bauer's illustrations for this tale are among the most famous in Swedish art.
moral: Innocence untempered by caution can be lost to the dangers of the world, piece by piece.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Sagan om älgtjuren Skutt och lilla prinsessan Tuvstarr' (1913) by Helge Kjellin, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Magpie with Salt on Her Tail
summary: A poor boy named Olle (or Theo in some versions) is always wishing for things he cannot afford — a knife, a sled, a horse and carriage. An old man tells him an old legend: if you sprinkle salt on a magpie's tail and make a wish before the salt falls off, the wish will come true. Olle spends days trying to catch a magpie, but they are too clever. Finally, a magpie speaks to him, claiming to be an enchanted prince. The magpie agrees to let Olle sprinkle salt on her tail, but only if he first brings her increasingly extravagant gifts — first a fine knife, then a bicycle, then a sled, then a horse and carriage, then a castle, and finally chests of gold. Each time Olle acquires the item through his own hard work, the magpie demands something grander. Olle's frustration grows, but he persists. Finally, after years of labor, he has earned the castle, the gold, and everything the magpie demanded. When at last he sprinkles the salt on her tail and she asks him to make his wish, Olle realizes he already has everything he could ever want — all earned through his own efforts. The magpie flies away, having taught him the greatest lesson of all. The story is a Swedish folk tale variation on the theme that hard work, not magic, is the true path to achieving one's dreams.
moral: The true path to achieving your wishes is not through magic but through your own hard work and perseverance.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Skatan som fick salt på stjärten' (1903) by Anna Wahlenberg.

title: The Flower of Happiness on Sunnymount Crest
summary: Deep in the Swedish forest, on the sun-drenched peak of Sunnymount Crest (Solberga klint), grows a magical flower that grants happiness to whoever finds it. Two young girls set out on a perilous journey through the deep woods where trolls, fairies, and witches keep their secrets. Their only hope of true happiness lies in reaching this elusive flower at the top of the mountain. Along the way, they encounter various supernatural beings from Swedish folklore — some helpful, some hostile. They must navigate enchanted forests, outwit dangerous creatures, and persevere through exhaustion and fear. The flower at Sunnymount Crest represents not just magical happiness but the idea that true fulfillment comes from the journey itself and the courage to pursue one's dreams despite all obstacles. The story, by Alfred Smedberg, was first published in the 1907 edition of 'Bland Tomtar och Troll' with illustrations by John Bauer. It reflects Smedberg's characteristic themes of love for the Swedish countryside, moral clarity, and the triumph of goodness and perseverance over adversity.
moral: The pursuit of happiness requires courage and perseverance through many obstacles.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Lyckoblomman på Solberga klint' (1907) by Alfred Smedberg, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: Dag and Daga, and the Flying Troll of Sky Mountain
summary: Dag and Daga are orphaned siblings who live alone in a forest cottage after their parents die. Despite being young, they manage to survive through their own resourcefulness: Dag hunts with his bow, and Daga gathers herbs, berries, and mushrooms. They live happily until one day Dag goes hunting and does not return. Daga waits in vain, then discovers only his hat and quiver left behind. Despite the warnings of neighbors who tell her to abandon hope, Daga sets out alone to find her brother. She travels through vast forests and over high mountains, facing dangers and exhaustion. Unbeknownst to her, her loyal house tomte follows and protects her. She encounters a prince who falls in love with her and offers her fine clothes and a golden crown, but when she realizes his servants are not actually searching for Dag, she leaves the finery behind and continues alone. She reaches Sky Mountain, where the terrible Flying Troll dwells. Inside, she finds Dag encased in rock — the troll imprisoned him for failing to learn gold-forging. The troll offers a bargain: if Daga can produce a queen's crown of pure gold before he counts to three, he will free Dag. As the troll begins counting, Daga produces the golden crown the prince gave her and throws it onto the troll's clawed hand. Stunned, the troll must keep his word, and Dag is freed. The reunited siblings return home, and the prince, having punished his dishonest servants, finds Daga and marries her, valuing her loyalty and bravery above all. The story celebrates sibling devotion, courage, and the power of keeping one's word.
moral: Loyalty and courage in the face of danger will ultimately triumph, and faithful love is rewarded.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Sagan om Dag och Daga och flygtrollet på Skyberget' (1907) by Harald Östenson, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: Linda-Gold and the Old King
summary: Long ago there lived an eccentric old king who had suffered great sorrow: his queen and children had died, and someone had torn his heart to pieces — someone 'with claws,' he said. Ever since, the king imagines that everyone has claws on their hands. No one is allowed to come within two arm's lengths of him. Servants must serve food at the farthest edge of the table, and anyone who steps closer spends a week in prison. Despite his oddities, he rules justly and is beloved by his people. But they worry because he refuses to remarry or name an heir. One day, a little girl named Linda-Gold arrives at the palace. Unlike everyone else, she is not afraid of the king. With the innocent audacity that only a child possesses, she approaches him and touches his hand. The king is so startled by her fearless touch that instead of ordering her imprisoned, he allows her to stay. Linda-Gold's pure-hearted love and her complete lack of fear gradually work their way into the king's wounded heart. She cuts her fingernails to show him she has no claws. Through her gentle presence, the king begins to heal from his trauma. His delusion fades, and he eventually adopts Linda-Gold as his daughter and heir, discovering that she truly has no claws — only the pure heart of a child. The story is a poignant exploration of grief, trauma, and the healing power of innocent love.
moral: Innocent love and fearless kindness can heal even the deepest emotional wounds.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Linda-Gull och den gamle kungen' (1903) by Anna Wahlenberg, in 'Länge, länge, sedan... Sagor'.

title: The Boy and the Trolls, or The Adventure
summary: An unnamed young lad packs his lunch, leaves the city, and heads into the woods in search of adventure. Meanwhile, in the kingdom, the young princess has gone missing, and her father the king has surrendered control of the realm to his second wife — a conniving woman suspected of orchestrating her stepdaughter's disappearance. On his journey, the boy encounters three troll brothers — Big Brother (with enormous ears), Medium Brother (with a huge chin), and Little Brother (with a prominent nose). Through his unfailingly polite manner, the boy treats them as he would any strangers, calling them 'uncle.' The trolls, who think the boy is hideous (to accentuate their own beauty), are heading home to their mother's mountain with treasures (silver, gold, precious stones) and delicacies (a garden snake, a frog, a toad) to court a captive princess. The boy follows them. Inside the mountain, the princess is being held captive by the troll mother until she chooses which son to marry. Benevolent elves bring fresh air and fruit, but cannot break the trolls' magic. The boy learns from the troll mother that the queen is actually her sister — a troll herself. The only thing that can defeat the trolls is a magic spell spoken by a fearless boy. The boy recites the words, rescues the princess, and they return to the city with the trolls' treasure. There, the evil queen is plotting to seal all windows and build a wall against the west wind. The boy exposes the queen as a troll, recites the magic words again, and the west wind blows the trolls away. The boy is rewarded, and the kingdom is saved.
moral: Courage, politeness, and a pure heart can overcome even the most fearsome adversaries.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Pojken och trollen eller Äventyret' (1915) by Walter Stenström, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Maiden in the Castle of Rosy Clouds
summary: A poor, hardworking young man (named Cedric in some versions) collapses from exhaustion and dreams of a beautiful fairy maiden who walks out of rosy clouds and invites him to find her. When he wakes, he is determined to win her. But no one knows where the Castle of Rosy Clouds is, and people mock him for his quest. Finally, an old woman tells him a poem revealing three magical items he must obtain: a gray stallion who can find his way through the clouds, a red mantle to protect him from fire, and a sword named Gull to split the iron serpent's skull. The young man has no idea how to acquire these items. He returns to ordinary life, but over many years, he encounters three situations where people are in danger. Each time he selflessly helps — rescuing someone from a snake, saving a town, and so on — and each heroic act rewards him with one of the three magical items. But the quest takes so long that by the time he finally reaches the Castle of Rosy Clouds, he is an old man. As he ascends through the clouds on the gray stallion, wielding the sword Gull and wearing the red mantle, a giant appears. He charges and the giant vanishes. At the castle, the fairy maiden welcomes him and, looking at his reflection in the moat, he sees that he has been made young and handsome again. They live happily together as rulers of the castle. The story celebrates perseverance, selflessness, and the idea that true love is worth a lifetime of effort.
moral: A lifetime of selfless deeds is the only path to winning true and lasting love.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Jungfrun i rosiga molnens borg' (1911) by Harald Östenson, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Queen
summary: Queen Adelgunda possesses a remarkable gift: she can see into the hearts of men. This power makes her a wise and just ruler, but it also creates complications. When the noble Sigmund — whom the queen secretly loves — is accused of dishonorable intentions, the queen's gift suggests his guilt. To save Sigmund from the dishonor that her veiled judgment implies, Adelgunda must make a painful choice: reveal her own love for him, risking her reputation and position, or allow an innocent man to be condemned. The story follows the queen's internal struggle as she balances her public duty with her private heart. In a climactic moment, she chooses love and truth over safety and appearances, baring her soul to save the man she loves. The story is notable for its psychological depth and focus on a female protagonist who holds real power and must navigate complex emotional and political terrain. Anna Wahlenberg, who wrote over 200 fairy tales, was known for embedding social commentary and feminist themes in her stories. 'The Queen' exemplifies her ability to combine traditional fairy tale enchantment with nuanced human emotion, portraying a ruler whose supernatural gift becomes a burden that only love can truly overcome.
moral: True love requires the courage to be vulnerable and the willingness to sacrifice for another.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Drottningen' (1903) by Anna Wahlenberg. Also appears in 'The Diamond Bird and Other Tales' (1969, Doubleday).

title: The Bird Grip
summary: A king goes blind, and an old woman tells him that only the song of the magical bird Grip — kept in a cage by a king in another land — can restore his sight. His eldest son sets out to fetch the bird but stops at a merry inn and forgets his mission, drinking and gambling. The second son does the same. The youngest son, however, resists temptation and continues his journey. He stays at a sinister inn where the host murders guests who cannot pay. The prince pays the dead man's debt, arranges his burial, and escapes with the help of a captive maid. He then meets a talking fox who becomes his guide. The fox gives him magic grains to steal the bird Grip, but warns him not to stroke it. The prince succeeds in taking the bird but strokes it anyway, causing it to scream. Captured, he is put on trial and, following the fox's advice, answers 'Yes' when asked if he is a master thief. The king spares him on condition that he steal the world's most beautiful princess, then a golden horse. Each time the prince succeeds with the fox's help (though he nearly fails each time by breaking the fox's warnings). After rescuing the princess and horse, the prince finds his jealous older brothers about to be hanged for debt. He ransoms them, but they betray him, throwing him into a lion pit and claiming the treasures for themselves. The bird will not sing, the horse will not be ridden, and the princess weeps constantly. The prince escapes the lions (the fox was there protecting him), beheads the fox (which transforms into the dead man whose debt he paid), and disguises himself as a horse-shoer. He fits the horse with golden shoes and, by calling the bird by name, makes it sing, restoring the king's sight. The truth is revealed, the brothers are banished, and the youngest marries the princess.
moral: Loyalty, perseverance, and kindness to strangers are rewarded, while betrayal and jealousy are punished.
source: Andrew Lang, The Pink Fairy Book (1897). Swedish folk tale, originally collected from Swedish oral tradition.

title: King Lindorm
summary: A childless queen receives advice from an old woman: bathe in running water and eat two red onions. In her excitement, she eats one onion with the skin on, and peels the other. As a result, she gives birth first to a hideous lindorm (serpent), who is secretly thrown into the forest, and then to a beautiful prince. Years later, when the prince sets out to find a bride, the lindorm blocks the road and demands that a mate be found for him first. The king and queen try to appease the lindorm by forcing women to marry him, but he devours each bride. Finally, a shepherd's daughter is offered. She wanders into the forest weeping and meets a wise old woman who tells her exactly what to do: she must wear all her dresses, and for each dress she removes, demand that the lindorm shed one of his skins. On her wedding night, the girl follows this advice. The lindorm sheds one skin after another, until nine skins lie on the floor and he is revealed as a handsome prince beneath — the true eldest son, freed from the curse caused by the queen eating the onion with its skin. The story has a complex second part: King Lindorm goes to war, leaving his queen with his evil stepmother, who writes that the queen gave birth to 'whelps.' The faithful servant hides the queen and her twin sons. After years of wandering, she stays with a man who has made a pact with the Devil, which she rescues using three magical nuts from her mother's grave. Eventually, King Lindorm returns, discovers the truth, and the family is reunited.
moral: Courage and wisdom can break even the most powerful curses.
source: Andrew Lang, The Pink Fairy Book (1897). Swedish variant collected by Eva Wigström from Landskrona. ATU type 433B.

title: The Sea Nymph
summary: One night on the northwest shores of the island of Gotland, fishermen sleeping in a hut see a white, dew-besprinkled woman's hand reaching through the door. They recognize it as a sea nymph seeking to destroy them, and pretend to be asleep. The next day, a young, newly married man from Kinnar in Lummelund joins them. He boasts that had he been present, he would have taken the hand. That night, a plump white arm with a beautiful hand reaches in. True to his word, the young man seizes it. Instantly, he is drawn silently out through the door and disappears. Despite an extensive search, he is not found. Three years pass, and his wife, having mourned him as dead, is persuaded to remarry. On the evening of her wedding, a stranger enters the cottage — it is her first husband, returned. He explains that he was dragged down to the sea nymph's pearly halls, where he forgot his former life completely. Only that morning, the sea nymph told him 'There will be a dusting out in Kinnar this evening,' meaning his wife's wedding. His memory returned, and he begged to see her. The sea nymph allowed him to go but forbade him from entering the house. Unable to resist, he entered anyway. A great tempest immediately rose, tearing off half the roof. The man fell sick and died three days later. The legend, collected by Herman Hofberg in 'Swedish Fairy Tales' (1890), is a classic cautionary tale about the dangers of supernatural beings, the consequences of arrogance, and the irrevocable nature of bargains made with otherworldly powers.
moral: Arrogance and rash promises can lead to terrible consequences, and some bargains with the supernatural cannot be undone.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales, translated by W.H. Myers (1890). Collected from Gotland, Sweden.

title: Ljungby Horn and Pipe
summary: At Trolle-Ljungby Castle in Scania, southern Sweden, a large glacial boulder called Maglestenen stands in the fields. Legend says that trolls live beneath this stone, and every Christmas Eve, the stone rises on golden pillars while the trolls feast and make merry beneath it. In the mid-16th century, the widowed Lady Sissela Ulfstand, mistress of Ljungby, grew curious about these tales and promised her best horse to any servant who would ride to Maglestenen on Christmas night and investigate. A brave stable hand took the challenge. When he arrived, he found the stone indeed raised on golden pillars, with trolls reveling beneath. They offered him a drinking horn and a pipe (whistle), urging him to drink the troll king's health and blow the pipe. As he was about to drink, a captive girl (a human taken by the trolls) secretly warned him. He threw the drink over his shoulder — the drops burned his horse's flank — and galloped away with the horn and pipe. The trolls chased him, but the girl had advised him to ride across a plowed field (forming a cross shape, which trolls cannot cross). The trolls were forced to run along the furrows, slowing them down. He reached the castle just in time, the drawbridge was raised, and the trolls could not enter. Furious, they cursed the estate: the horse and rider would die, the castle would burn three times, and if the artifacts were ever removed, the owner would face great misfortune. The horn (a drinking vessel with gilded silver mounts) and the pipe (carved from ivory, playable from both ends) remain at Trolle-Ljungby to this day. The curse is said to have come true: the horse died, the rider died three days later, and the castle burned three times.
moral: Some treasures come at too great a cost, and ancient curses should not be taken lightly.
source: Swedish folk legend from Scania, recorded by Wilhelm Julius Coyet (1692) and by Carl Linnaeus in his 'Skånska resa' (1750). Artifacts preserved at Trolle-Ljungby Castle.

title: The Brownies (Tomtar)
summary: In Swedish folklore, tomtar (also called brownies or nisses) are small, elderly-looking men about the size of a three- or four-year-old child, dressed in gray clothes with red caps. They live on farms, usually in barns or pantries, and work tirelessly to maintain the household. They wake servants for threshing, help in forges by signaling when to turn the iron, and watch over the cattle. Where there is order and prosperity, the tomte thrives; where there is disrespect or uncleanliness, he leaves. One story tells of a farmer whose harvest never ran out. Hiding in the barn, he saw a crowd of tomtar each carrying a stalk of rye, including one tiny tomte no bigger than a thumb carrying a straw. When the farmer asked why the smallest one puffed so hard under such a light load, luck left his house forever. Another story tells of a tomte who was offered Christmas porridge with honey. When the porridge was too hot and the honey melted to the bottom, he thought there was no honey and killed a cow in anger. Finding the honey at the bottom of the bowl, he bitterly regretted his haste and stole an identical cow from a neighbor to replace the one he killed. Yet another tale describes a housewife whose meal bins never emptied. Peeking through the door, she saw a tomte sifting meal tirelessly. Feeling grateful, she made him new clothes and left them on the bin. When he tried to sift while wearing his fine new suit, the meal made him dusty. He threw down the sieve and declared, 'Junker Grand is dusting himself. He shall sift no more,' and all his blessings ceased. These stories teach that one should never call attention to the brownies' work or try to reward them openly.
moral: Respect the unseen helpers who maintain good fortune, and never question the gifts of luck.
source: Swedish folk tradition. Collected in Herman Hofberg, 'Swedish Fairy Tales' (1890) and various Swedish folklore collections.

title: Kópakonan (The Seal Woman of Mikladalur)
summary: A fisherman named Torkil from Mikladalur on Kalsoy Island watches a group of seal-people dancing on the shore after shedding their sealskins. He falls in love with the most beautiful seal-woman, steals her sealskin, and locks it in a chest. Unable to return to the sea, she marries him and bears his children. Years later, Torkil forgets the key to the chest while fishing; Kópakonan finds her skin and returns to the ocean, leaving her human family behind. She later appears in Torkil's dream begging him not to kill her seal husband and children, but he ignores her. Kópakonan curses the men of Mikladalur to die at sea and fall from cliffs until enough dead surround Kalsoy holding hands.

moral: Respect the natural world and its boundaries.
source: Visit Faroe Islands; Guide to Faroe Islands; The Adventure Seekers

title: Risin og Kellingin (The Giant and the Witch)
summary: Two giant sea stacks off the northern coast of Eysturoy, near Eiði. Legend says that giants in Iceland grew envious of the Faroe Islands and sent a giant and his wife (a witch) to drag the islands back to Iceland. The giant stood in the sea while the witch climbed the mountain Eiðiskollur with a heavy rope to tie the islands together. They struggled all night but could not move the islands. At dawn, sunlight struck them and turned them both to stone. The 71m stack (Risin/the Giant) and 68m stack (Kellingin/the Witch) remain visible from Tjørnuvík on Streymoy, staring longingly west toward Iceland.

title: Nykur (The Nix of Sørvágsvatn)
summary: Nykur is a shapeshifting water spirit that inhabits lakes, most famously Sørvágsvatn on Vágar Island. It appears most often as a beautiful white horse that lures people onto its back, then gallops into the lake to drown them. A popular tale tells of two brothers playing at the lakeshore. The older brother climbed onto the horse, but the younger called his brother by name—'Nikkolas'—which sounded like 'Nix.' Hearing his own name, the water horse lost power and retreated into the lake, saving both boys. Nykur can also appear in other forms. An illuminated sculpture by Póla Skarðenni was installed at Sørvágsvatn in 2017 to commemorate the legend.

title: Huldufólk (The Hidden People)
summary: Huldufólk are supernatural beings in Faroese folklore described as large-built, grey-clad, dark-haired people who live in mounds, rocks, and cliffs—invisible to humans unless they choose to be seen. They are tricksters who dislike electricity, technology, crosses, and churches. According to legend, they left Suðuroy when electricity arrived in the 1950s. The Hidden People feature in Faroese nursery rhymes (skjaldur) such as 'Hulda, mulda, tulda' and traditional kvæði (ballads). They are said to fear knives and can be warded off with the sign of the cross. They coexist in a parallel society with farms, churches, and social structures mirroring human communities.

source: Local.fo; The Dockyards; Wikipedia - Huldufólk; Stephen Pax Leonard (2010)

title: Marmennilin (The Merman of Elduvík)
summary: A farmer named Arnfinn from Elduvík encountered a Marmennil—a merman who lives at the bottom of the sea, smaller than a human with long fingers, who delights in teasing fishermen by stealing bait and snagging hooks. One day, the merman tried to stick Arnfinn's hook to the seabed but accidentally hooked his own hand and was pulled into the boat. The fishermen made the sign of the cross and brought him home. For years, they took Marmennilin fishing; when fish were below, he would laugh and play, and the men always caught plenty. But one day in rough waves, the men forgot to make the sign of the cross, and Marmennilin escaped into the sea forever. A statue by Hans Pauli Olsen was unveiled in 2020.

title: Rinkusteinar (The Rocking Stones of Oyndarfjørður)
summary: Two large boulders rest at the shore near Oyndarfjørður, constantly rocking back and forth with the rhythm of the waves. Legend says that a local sorceress (witch) saw pirate ships approaching her village after they had already attacked Fuglafjørður, robbing and killing. She rushed to the shore and cast a spell, transforming the pirate ships into stone. The two rocking stones are the petrified vessels, eternally rocking in the waves but never able to reach the village. Though often called Viking ships in modern sources, the raiders were actually Irish pirates. The natural phenomenon of their motion continues to baffle visitors.

title: Ormur (The Feared Farmer of Skála)
summary: Ormur ('Worm') was a notoriously savage farmer in Skála, a giant of a man who stole sheep from neighboring farms. When farmer Pætur of Funningur caught him, Ormur forced him to swear silence under threat of his axe. Later, Pætur visited Ormur's home and was served sheep heads—but Ormur had forgotten to remove the ears, which bore Pætur's identifying marks. When Pætur confronted him, Ormur sharpened his axe. Pætur escaped, knocking a corn rack into the fireplace and burning Ormur's house down. In another incident, Ormur killed farmer Jógvan from Oyri and his son at Tippafossur after calling on the devil. He was eventually caught, his Achilles' tendon severed by a thrown knife, and executed. He is buried at Stevnuválur overlooking the lands where he committed his crimes.

title: Snæbjørn (Snow Bear of Suðuroy)
summary: Snæbjørn was a powerful, brave, and proud servant working for a wealthy farmer in Hvalba on Suðuroy. He was secretly in love with a milkmaid. When a Dutch ship illegally traded luxury goods, Snæbjørn bought four silk scarves for her. The judge sentenced him to four years imprisonment on Bremerholm in Denmark for this crime. Enraged, Snæbjørn struck at the judge but accidentally killed the bailiff instead. He fled into the mountains where his speed, climbing skill, and knowledge of the terrain made capture impossible. Villagers secretly helped him with food and warned him of search parties. He became a folk hero—an outlaw who defied unjust Danish authority, reminiscent of Robin Hood.

title: Trøllanes and the Troll House Party
summary: The settlement of Trøllanes on Kalsoy Island takes its name from ancient trolls who would come ashore once a year to wreak havoc—eating all available food, drinking, dancing, and behaving like wild party-goers. Terrified villagers would flee to Mikladalur every year, leaving their homes to be ransacked. One year, a woman was unable to escape and hid under a table as the trolls danced and feasted above her. In terror, she exclaimed 'Christ!' The trolls, hearing the holy name, fled from the village in panic and never returned. The route to the Kallur lighthouse begins in Trøllanes, offering some of the most dramatic views in the Faroe Islands.

title: Trøllkonufingur (The Troll Woman's Finger)
summary: A giant troll-woman from Iceland came to Vágar Island with the same plan as Risin and Kellingin: to steal the Faroe Islands and drag them to Iceland. Like the giants, she could only work at night. Caught by sunrise, sunlight turned her to stone. She toppled from the cliff into the sea—and was so enormous that her head became the island of Koltur (inhabited by only two people, Bjørn and Lukka Patursson), while one finger remained above water as the rock formation Trøllkonufingur near Sandavagur. Visible from the airport, it is a popular climbing spot. In 1844, a member of the Royal Danish entourage climbed it, descended, realized he'd dropped a glove, climbed back up—and was never seen again.

title: Tórur Rami and the Three Gifts of Mykines
summary: A giant named Tórur Rami tried to conquer the island of Mykines. The native inhabitant Óli Rami opposed him. When the giant approached, Óli Rami used magic to split Mykineshólmur from the main island, creating the islet now reached by a narrow metal bridge. The giant jumped across and they fought. Óli Rami won and spared the giant's life in exchange for three gifts: (1) a great whale would appear annually in Hvalagjógv bay—but the people complained and it stopped; (2) a giant magical tree—but the people complained it was shabby and it withered; (3) gannets (seabirds) that still nest only on Mykineshólmur. The condition was that the people must never complain about the gifts.

title: Grímur Kamban (The First Settler)
summary: Grímur Kamban is recorded in the Færeyinga Saga as the first Scandinavian settler in the Faroe Islands, arriving around 825 AD—decades before the main Norse colonization of Iceland. He settled at Funningur on Eysturoy. The name 'Kamban' may be of Gaelic origin, suggesting he was a Norse-Gael from the British Isles (possibly the Hebrides or Ireland). According to legend, he was a feared Viking who fled Norway after committing murder. His settlement at Funningur marks the beginning of continuous habitation in the Faroes. The story bridges history and folklore, representing the arrival of humans into a landscape already inhabited by trolls, giants, and hidden people.

title: Tróndur í Gøtu (Tróndur the Viking Chieftain)
summary: Tróndur í Gøtu was a powerful 10th-century Viking chieftain who lived in Gøta on Eysturoy. He is the central figure in the Færeyinga Saga, which recounts his fierce opposition to the Christianization of the islands. He led pagan resistance against Sigmundur Brestisson, who was sent by King Olaf Tryggvason of Norway to convert the Faroese. After a series of battles, Sigmundur ultimately won and killed Tróndur. However, in folklore, Tróndur is often portrayed as a tragic hero—a symbol of Faroese independence and resistance against foreign domination. His burial mound is said to exist near Gøta.

title: Símun of Kirkjubøur (Hulda-Símun and the Hidden Woman)
summary: Símun Símunarson, nicknamed Hulda-Símun or Troll-Símun, was a massive farmer at Kirkjubøur on Streymoy. A hulduman (hidden woman) lived on Kirkjubøur Felltop. The legend tells how Símun was kidnapped by the Huldufólk and taken to the cliffs near Kirkjubøur. After his encounter with the Hidden People, the Huldufólk were never seen in the village again. Símun was described as so large that his jacket required three lengths of double-width cloth, and none of his farmhands dared walk ahead of him on sheep round-ups. The story reflects Faroese beliefs about the Hidden People dwelling in the landscape and their ability to interact with humans.
full_text: Símun Símunarson, nicknamed Hulda-Símun or Troll-Símun, was a massive farmer at Kirkjubøur on Streymoy. A hulduman (hidden woman) lived on Kirkjubøur Felltop. The legend tells how Símun was kidnapped by the Huldufólk and taken to the cliffs near Kirkjubøur. After his encounter with the Hidden People, the Huldufólk were never seen in the village again. Símun was described as so large that his jacket required three lengths of double-width cloth, and none of his farmhands dared walk ahead of him on sheep round-ups. The story reflects Faroese beliefs about the Hidden People dwelling in the landscape and their ability to interact with humans.
moral: Folklore preserves the wisdom of a people.
source: John F. West - Faroese Folk-tales & Legends (1980); The Adventure Seekers

title: The Troll Who Captured a Princess
summary: A classic Faroese folk tale collected by Jakob Jakobsen tells of a troll who lived in a cave and captured a princess to pick lice from his hair. Three brothers set out to rescue her. The elder two brothers failed, but the youngest—helped by the princess who threw him a ball of yarn to track the cave entrance—confronted the troll. The troll tried to trick the boy but was outwitted. When the troll gained entry to the king's house by picking the Faroese wooden lock (hvölpalás), the boy killed him. The treacherous brothers claimed credit, but the princess revealed the truth. The youngest brother married the princess and became king. Faroese trolls are creatures of darkness, large and ugly but easily outwitted.

title: Kópakonan's Curse (The Seal Woman's Revenge)
summary: The darker sequel to the Kópakonan legend. After the seal woman returned to the sea, her human husband Torkil began sealing (hunting seals). Kópakonan appeared to him in a dream, begging him not to kill her seal husband and children. Torkil ignored her warning. When he killed her seal family, Kópakonan rose from the sea and pronounced a terrible curse: the men of Mikladalur would die at sea and fall from the cliffs until enough dead men could encircle the entire island of Kalsoy holding hands. The curse is said to still haunt the village, where the sea claims lives with unusual frequency. A 2.6-meter bronze statue of Kópakonan by Hans Pauli Olsen stands at the water's edge in Mikladalur since 2014.

title: The Creation of the Faroe Islands
summary: Two folk explanations exist for the archipelago's creation. The first says the islands were formed from dirt that gathered under God's fingernails while He created the world and flicked away. The second, more dramatic myth connects to Norse mythology: after the World Serpent Jörmungandr (child of Loki) was defeated, a tooth fell from the dying serpent's mouth into the ocean and became the Faroe Islands. Both accounts are recorded in Faroese oral tradition. The Jörmungandr version connects Faroese identity to the great Norse mythological cycle, while the nail-dirt tale echoes a humble self-perception common in island folklore.

title: Huldufólk Captured by a Priest at Sørvágsvatn
summary: At Sørvágsvatn Lake on Vágar (the lake that famously appears to hang over the ocean), there is a stone mound where, according to legend, a priest trapped several Huldufólk (Hidden People). The priest used his spiritual authority to imprison the supernatural beings within the mound. Visitors to the area claim they can still hear the wailing and cries of the trapped hidden people emanating from the stone. The legend reflects Faroese beliefs about the power of Christian clergy over pagan supernatural beings, and the common motif of Huldufólk being afraid of crosses, churches, and Christian symbols.

title: Nykur and the Brothers (Lake Vatnsdalsvatn Variant)
summary: A variant of the Nykur legend told around Lake Vatnsdalsvatn. Two children were playing by the lakeshore when they saw a beautiful white horse grazing nearby. The older brother, being tall enough, climbed onto the horse's back. Immediately the horse began galloping toward the deep water. The younger brother, who only wanted his sibling to come back and play, called out his brother's name. Because the Nykur loses its power when its name or the rider's name is spoken, the horse stopped and retreated, and the brother was saved. The tale serves as a warning to children about the dangers of lakes and the importance of knowing how to counter supernatural threats. Speaking a water spirit's name breaks its spell in Faroese folklore.

title: Draugur (Faroese Revenant Legends)
summary: In Faroese folklore, the draugur (dreygur) is a malevolent undead being or revenant, akin to the Norse draugr. Draugar are described as large in build, wearing grey clothes with black hair—the same description given for Huldufólk in Faroese tradition. They are said to inhabit graves, mounds, and coastal areas, often guarding treasure or haunting the living. Unlike ghosts, draugar have physical bodies—animated corpses that can swell to enormous size. In Faroese sea folklore, drowned fishermen may become sea-draugar who haunt the waters where they died. The draugur embodies the thin line between the living and dead in isolated island communities where death at sea was a constant reality.

title: The Sorceress of Oyndarfjørður
summary: The Rinkusteinar legend in its more detailed form. Long ago, when Irish pirates attacked Fuglafjørður, they killed, robbed, and terrorized the village before setting sail for Oyndarfjørður. An old witch (sorceress) living in Oyndarfjørður saw them coming and rushed to the shore. She raised her arms and cast a powerful curse, transforming the pirate ships into stone. The two rocking stones (rinkusteinar) are the pirate vessels, forever rocking in the waves near Oyndarfjørður, eternally prevented from reaching their destination. The witch's name has been lost to history. The story is one of the best-known witch legends in the Faroe Islands.

title: Faroese Chain Dance and Kvæði (Ballad Tradition)
summary: The Faroese chain dance (Føroya Dansur) accompanied by kvæði (ballads) is the national dance and a living tradition that preserves folklore. Dancers form a circle holding hands, moving rhythmically while a kvæðamaður (ballad singer) recites verses recounting tales of legendary heroes, folklore figures, and historical events. Many kvæði date to the Middle Ages and preserve stories otherwise lost, including the Sjúrðar Kvæði (cyclic ballads about Sigurd the Dragon-Slayer from Norse mythology) and ballads about Finnur hin Fróði and other Faroese figures. The tradition was instrumental in preserving the Faroese language during centuries of Danish rule and continues as a living cultural practice at festivals and gatherings.

title: Tippafossur and the Killing of Jógvan
summary: A grim legend within the Ormur cycle. Jógvan, a farmer from Oyri, caught Ormur of Skála stealing his sheep and attacked him. Miraculously, Jógvan overpowered the huge Ormur and held him down, calling for his son to bring a knife. But the son, terrified of Ormur, fled and hid at the Tippafossur gorge. Ormur, pinned and fearing death, promised the devil a piece of his little finger in exchange for strength. Suddenly gaining supernatural power, Ormur broke free and killed Jógvan. He then found the boy at Tippafossur and killed him too. Later, in his sleep, Ormur muttered: 'The clothes lie under the corn and the bodies under Tippafossur.' His farmhands heard, found the evidence, and informed the authorities.

title: The Huldufólk Nursery Rhyme: Hulda, Mulda, Tulda
summary: A traditional Faroese skjaldur (short nursery rhyme or tale) that invokes the Huldufólk. The rhyme 'Hulda, mulda, tulda' is a charm asking the Hidden People not to hide lost objects. It reflects the common belief that Huldufólk, being mischievous tricksters, enjoy hiding household items from humans. The rhyme tradition is one of the few surviving forms of Huldufólk interaction in the modern era—residents of Suðuroy claim the hidden people left with the introduction of electricity in the 1950s. These skjaldur, studied by linguist Stephen Pax Leonard, represent an endangered oral tradition unique to the Faroe Islands, blending pre-Christian and Christian elements.

title: Finnur hin Fróði and Faroese Wizard Legends
summary: Faroese folklore includes tales of powerful wizards and cunning folk (wise men and women) who could control weather, heal sickness, and ward off trolls. One of the most famous is Finnur hin Fróði (Finn the Wise), a legendary figure associated with magical knowledge and runic wisdom. These wizard legends blend Norse pagan traditions with later Christian elements—wizards were said to possess books of black magic (the 'Black Book' tradition) that could only be passed on to a willing successor. The island of Svínoy and the village of Sumba are particularly associated with wizard lore. These tales reflect the historical tension between official Christianity and older folk beliefs that persisted for centuries.

title: The Sjörå – Lady of the Baltic Sea
summary: The sjörå (sea spirit) is a guardian entity of the Baltic waters surrounding the Åland archipelago. She appears as a beautiful woman, sometimes described as hollow-backed like the skogsrå, who can calm storms or raise tempests depending on how she is treated. Fishermen and sailors traditionally greeted her with respect before setting out. Those who showed her reverence were rewarded with calm seas and good catches, while those who disrespected her faced endless wind and perilous voyages. The sjörå embodies the deep bond between the Åland islanders and the sea that has sustained them for millennia. Stone cairns along the shores of Eckerö, Finström, and Geta mark ancient graves of seafarers — each a monument to lives carried across these treacherous waters. Even today, some islanders speak of her presence when the tide falls silent and the horizon blurs.
source: https://mythicnorthproject.blogspot.com/2025/11/aland-isles-between-worlds.html

title: The Storm-Witches of Kastelholm
summary: Åland folklore tells of storm-witches who possessed the secret art of 'tying the wind' into knots of rope. These women could sell sailors a favorable breeze or calm at sea by selling them a knotted rope: untying one knot released a light wind, untying two filled the sails, but untying all three would awaken a devastating tempest. The practice is associated with the maritime culture of Kastelholm Castle, the 14th-century fortress at the heart of the main island. Even today, some islanders refuse to throw rope knots into the sea without cutting them first, for fear of stirring what should sleep. This legend reflects the deep folk belief in weather magic, a tradition found throughout the Nordic coastal regions but with a distinctly Åland character rooted in the archipelago's dependence on safe sea passage.
source: https://mythicnorthproject.blogspot.com/2025/11/aland-isles-between-worlds.html

title: The Kastelholm Witch Trials (1665–1668)
summary: The Kastelholm witch trials were the largest witch trials in Finnish history, held at Kastelholm Castle in Åland between 1665 and 1668. Governor Nils Psilander, educated at the University of Dorpat (Tartu) where witchcraft demonology was a major subject, conducted the trials. Unlike typical Finnish witch trials which involved single male defendants accused of sorcery, the Kastelholm trials were a mass trial modeled on continental European patterns. Several women were accused of attending a Witches' Sabbath and making a pact with the Devil. Six women were ultimately executed. The trials were unique in Finland for their scale and their focus on diabolical witchcraft rather than traditional folk magic. The first woman executed was Karin Persdotter, and the events have since inspired the 2016 Finnish film 'Devil's Bride' (Tulen morsian).
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kastelholm_witch_trials

title: The Devil's Dance at Bomarsund (Djävulsdansen)
summary: One of Åland's most famous legends, the Devil's Dance tells of a masked ball at the Russian military settlement of Skarpans near Bomarsund in the 1830s–1840s. A mysterious, elegantly dressed stranger arrived at the dance and asked which woman was the finest dancer. A priest's wife stepped forward. The stranger danced with her so wildly that blood came through her shoes. When the musicians stopped playing, the fiddles continued on their own. Finally, a clergyman named Prosten Sadelin was summoned. He confronted the stranger, who revealed a horse's hoof instead of a foot — the Devil himself. Sadelin drove the Devil out through a small hole in a window casement. The legend has inspired three theatrical plays and an opera performed at Alandica in Mariehamn in 2022.
source: https://granlundslada.blogspot.com/2022/12/djavulsdansen-i-bomarsund-tre.html

title: The Skogsrå – Forest Spirit of Åland's Woodlands
summary: The skogsrå (forest spirit) is a female nature spirit found throughout Swedish-speaking regions including Åland. She appears as a beautiful woman from the front, but seen from behind she has a hollow, rotten back and often a cow's tail or one horse foot. She lures men deep into the forests, leading them astray into swamps or wilderness. Men who follow her may lose their health or sanity after repeated visits. However, she is known to be kind to charcoal burners and woodcutters, and can reward hunters with good fortune. In the Christian reinterpretation common to Åland and the Nordic region, the skogsrå and similar rå spirits were said to be angels who fell from heaven with Lucifer — those who landed in forests became skogsrå, those in water became sjörå. The Åland archipelago, with its blend of pine forests and rocky shores, is considered a natural home for these borderland spirits.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skogsr%C3%A5

title: The Näcken – Water Fiddler of Åland's Lakes and Streams
summary: The Näcken (Näkk in Finnish Swedish) is a male water spirit who sits naked among the reeds or under waterfalls, playing hauntingly beautiful violin music. His playing is irresistibly seductive, luring listeners — especially children and young women — toward the water, where they drown. However, the Näcken can be outwitted by those who know the proper countermeasures: if someone learns to play a tune the Näcken cannot match, or if a priest thwarts him with prayer, the spirit can be driven away. In some versions, the Näcken teaches his art to those who bring him a stolen offering. In the Åland context, with its thousands of lakes, inlets, and streams, the Näcken was a powerful cautionary figure used to keep children away from dangerous waters. The figure is closely related to the Norwegian Fossegrim and the Finnish Näkki.
source: https://visitsweden.com/what-to-do/culture-history-and-art/culture/mythological-creatures

title: The Sea Draugr – Ghost of Drowned Sailors
summary: In the Nordic maritime tradition that extends to Åland, the draugr or sea draug is the restless spirit of a drowned sailor. It appears as a figure in oilskins with a head of seaweed, sailing in half a boat. The sea draugr serves as an omen of impending drowning: if a fisherman sees the draug or hears its cries, his own vessel is likely to be lost. The draugr could be placated with offerings — in the old Christmas tradition, beer was left out as 'draug-beer' to keep these spirits from becoming malicious. For Åland, where seafaring has been the backbone of life for thousands of years and where entire communities have lost men to the sea, the draugr legend gave voice to collective grief and the ever-present danger of the Baltic. The tradition of leaving offerings at sea cairns on islands like Eckerö and Geta connects to this belief in appeasing the drowned.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nordic_folklore

title: The Tomtegubbe – Åland's Household Guardian
summary: The tomte (or gårdstomte) is a household spirit who lives on farms and in barns, watching over the family and livestock. On Åland, as in the rest of the Swedish-speaking world, the tomte was both protector and disciplinarian: he rewarded hard work and good management with prosperity, but punished laziness, drunkenness, and night work with mischief or outright harm. The tomte expected to be fed — typically a bowl of porridge with butter on Christmas Eve. If neglected, he would cause accidents, make the cows go dry, or leave the farm entirely. The tradition is preserved in the annual children's anthology 'Bland tomtar och troll' (Among Gnomes and Trolls), first published in 1907 and still going today, which features stories collected from across the Swedish language area including Åland. The tomte represents the moral code of the traditional Åland farming community, where hard work and respect for the unseen were paramount.
source: https://www.isof.se/arkiv-och-insamling/digitala-arkivtjanster/sagenkartan

title: The Bäckahästen – The Brook Horse of Åland's Waters
summary: The bäckahästen (brook horse) is a shapeshifting water spirit that appears as a beautiful, seemingly tame horse near streams, lakes, or the sea. When someone climbs onto its back, the horse gallops into the water and drowns its rider. The legend was particularly used to warn children away from dangerous waters. In some versions, the bäckahästen can be tricked by throwing a piece of steel or iron over its head, forcing it to revert to its true form. This creature overlaps with the Näcken tradition and is closely related to the Celtic kelpie. The countless inlets, streams, and shallow coastal waters of the Åland archipelago made this a particularly relevant cautionary tale for island children. The creature appears numerous times in the Sagenkartan entries from the Swedish-speaking regions of Finland.
source: https://www.so-rummet.se/kategorier/religion/folktro

title: The Jomala Runestone – Odin's Mark on Åland
summary: The Jomala runestone, located at Jomala Church — the oldest stone church in Finland — is one of the most significant Viking-age artifacts on Åland. The runestone bears inscriptions invoking Odin's guidance and names voyagers who once crossed the Baltic. Its serpent forms curl around the old Younger Futhark alphabet in the classic runestone style of 11th-century Scandinavia. The stone represents Åland's role as a crossroads between Sweden and Finland during the Viking Age, when the islands served as vital waystations for sea travelers. Local folklore holds that the stone possesses protective powers and that disturbing it would bring ill fortune. Jomala Church itself, built atop earlier pagan sacred ground, represents the layering of Christian and pre-Christian belief that characterizes so much of Åland's folk tradition.
source: https://mythicnorthproject.blogspot.com/2025/11/aland-isles-between-worlds.html

title: The Saltvik Runestone – Serpent of the Norse Sea
summary: The Saltvik runestone is a second significant Viking-age monument on Åland, located in the Saltvik parish north of Mariehamn. Like the Jomala stone, it is carved with runic inscriptions in the Younger Futhark, its serpentine bands curling like waves around the ancient alphabet. The stone marks the connection between Åland's Viking-era settlers and the wider Norse world, commemorating voyages, kinship, and courage. In local folk belief, runestones were not mere memorials but living objects with spiritual power. Some Åland traditions held that runestones could speak at midnight, or that they protected the land from evil spirits. The Saltvik stone, in particular, is associated with the legend of the Devil's Dance — the parish church at Saltvik being the home of the priest's wife who allegedly danced with the Devil at Bomarsund.
source: https://mythicnorthproject.blogspot.com/2025/11/aland-isles-between-worlds.html

title: The Midsummer Bonfires – Ancient Solar Rites on Åland
summary: Every June, when the sun barely sets at this northern latitude, the people of Åland gather for Midsummer's Eve (Midsommar) — one of the most important celebrations of the year. Across the archipelago, bonfires blaze on hilltops and beaches, echoing solar rites far older than Christianity. These fires honor the eternal return of the sun, mark the height of fertility and growth, and ward off malevolent spirits. Couples dance around the midsummer pole (midsommarstång) decorated with birch leaves and wildflowers, while musicians play nyckelharpa, accordion, and fiddle in rhythms passed down through generations. In folk belief, Midsummer night was a time when the boundaries between worlds grew thin: the skogsrå and sjörå were particularly active, and magical plants gathered at midnight held special powers. Young women would pick seven different flowers and place them under their pillow to dream of their future husbands. The tradition remains one of the strongest living folk practices on Åland.
source: https://mythicnorthproject.blogspot.com/2025/11/aland-isles-between-worlds.html

title: The Vittra – The Hidden People of Åland
summary: The vittra (singular: vitter) are an underground-dwelling people who live in a parallel civilization beneath the hills and meadows of Åland. Invisible to most humans, they have their own farms, livestock, and churches. They are known to do favors for humans who treat them with dignity and respect, but can become vengeful if their homes are disturbed — for example, by building a structure on their invisible property. The vittra are closely related to the Swedish 'de underjordiska' (the underground ones) and the Scandinavian 'huldrefolk' (hidden folk). Offerings of food, particularly porridge, were traditionally left for them. In the Sagenkartan records from Åland, many entries describe encounters with vittra who borrowed tools from farmers or requested that specific areas be left undisturbed. The tradition reflects a deep-seated belief that the land is shared with unseen inhabitants who must be respected.
source: https://www.isof.se/arkiv-och-insamling/digitala-arkivtjanster/sagenkartan

title: The Älvor – Meadow Elves of the Åland Archipelago
summary: The älvor (elves) are beautiful, otherworldly female beings who dance in the meadows and misty fields of Åland, particularly at dawn and dusk. Their dancing leaves behind 'älvringar' (fairy rings) — circles of flattened grass or mushrooms where the ground is forever enchanted. Humans who interrupt their dances may be lured into their realm, losing all sense of time and place. Those who step into an älvring may become invisible to other humans or fall into a sleep from which they cannot wake. In Swedish-speaking Åland tradition, the älvor are skilled in magic and illusions. They are sometimes described as half-transparent spirits, closely linked to the coastal mists that roll in from the Baltic. The figure of the älva is related to the pre-Christian female deities called dís (plural dísir), powerful spirits associated with fate and protection. Even today, the word 'dis' remains a synonym for mist or very light rain in Swedish.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nordic_folklore

title: King Erik XIV's Imprisonment at Kastelholm
summary: In the autumn of 1571, the deposed Swedish King Erik XIV was imprisoned at Kastelholm Castle by his brother Johan III. Erik had been dethroned in 1568 and was moved between various castles before being held on Åland. Local legend holds that Erik's ghost still wanders the halls of Kastelholm, his footsteps echoing through the stone corridors at night. The imprisonment at Kastelholm is part of a larger body of historical folklore surrounding the castle, which first appears in written records in 1388. The castle has witnessed sieges, royal visits, witch trials, and fires — and each layer of its history has contributed to its reputation as one of the most haunted places in Åland. During the restoration of the castle in the 20th century, workers reported unexplained sounds and cold drafts, which some attribute to the restless spirits of its many historical occupants.
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kastelholm_Castle

title: The Russian Fortress of Bomarsund – A Cursed Ruin
summary: The unfinished fortress of Bomarsund, built by Russia between 1832 and 1854, is one of Åland's most evocative historical sites. Local folklore holds that the fortress was cursed from the start — built on ground that was never meant to be fortified. Thousands of laborers, prisoners, and soldiers worked on the project, which was to be the largest construction in Åland's history. But in August 1854, during the Crimean War, an Anglo-French fleet destroyed the fortress in just three and a half days. The defeat led to the demilitarization of Åland (which remains in effect today). Folk tradition speaks of strange lights seen among the ruins, ghostly Russian soldiers still marching their posts, and the phantom sounds of construction echoing from the hills. The nearby hilltop Djävulsberget ('Devil's Mountain') takes its name from the folklore surrounding the site. The Bomarsund legend exemplifies how a historical event became woven into the supernatural fabric of Åland's folk tradition.
source: https://aland.travel/bomarsund-en
